author: Daniel Bracher
tagline: A living portfolio. Every project documented — and the web ones running, right here in the page.
intro: Hand-built software, from a Rust spreadsheet engine to five rule-breaking arcade games. Each card below opens a full breakdown: what it is, how it works, and how to run it. Where a project runs in a browser, you can play with it live.

repos.name: SpatialWave
repos.tagline: A spatial productivity system — your project becomes a navigable map, and a deterministic Momentum Path shows the best next route through the work.
repos.summary: Not a board, list, or calendar. Goals, tasks, blockers, milestones and decisions live as connected nodes on a zoomable canvas, and the app continuously computes where to go next. The differentiator is momentum-based navigation: most tools show what exists; SpatialWave shows where work is blocked and the highest-leverage next step — with no AI, just a graph algorithm running client-side. Built with React + TypeScript + React Flow, and designed read-light for Firebase's free Spark plan.
repos.platform: Web — runs live in your browser

screenshots.alt: A spatial map of project nodes with a highlighted momentum path

live.note: The live SpatialWave app — the guest demo, running right here in the page. Drag nodes, connect dependencies, and toggle Focus to see the Momentum Path.

howItWorks: Your work is a graph: goals, tasks, blockers, milestones and decisions are nodes; directed links declare what must happen before what.

The Momentum Path engine scores every actionable node by priority × deadline pressure × leverage (how much it unblocks), then greedily simulates completion to produce an ordered route: do this, which unlocks that.
All of that is pure, dependency-free logic in src/lib — the path, the blocked set, and cycle detection — covered by a Vitest suite, independent of any pixels.
React Flow renders the canvas from a single Zustand store; Focus mode dims everything off-path; completing a node ripples a wave to its dependents.
Spark-safe by design: the whole workspace persists as one Firestore document (load = 1 read, save = 1 debounced write) in Phase 2.

run.label: Run locally
run.code: git clone https://github.com/danieldevelopes-collab/spatialwave && cd spatialwave && npm install && npm run dev

run.label: Test the engine
run.code: npm test   # 14 tests over the graph + momentum logic

repos.name: brick-innit
repos.tagline: One engine. Five brick-breakers — each one breaks a rule the genre spent fifty years assuming you couldn't.
repos.summary: A brick-breaker is one of gaming's oldest forms. brick-innit is five of them sharing a single reusable engine, each built to break one assumption nobody questioned: the wall comes alive (Conway's Game of Life), the ball turns to light, gravity bends every shot, you take a single planned shot, or bricks only break on the beat. Vanilla HTML + JavaScript, no build step, 100% offline — every sound is synthesised live with the Web Audio API.
repos.platform: Web — runs live in your browser

screenshots.alt: The brick-innit menu — five mode cards, each in its own accent colour

howItWorks: One reusable engine — Ball, Paddle, BrickGrid, circle-vs-rect collision, a particle field and a tiny Web-Audio Synth — composed five completely different ways.
Each game is a 'mode' implementing setup() / update(dt) / render(ctx) and registering itself; that single seam is why five wildly different games share ~600 lines of engine.
Plain classic-script JavaScript on a window.Brick namespace, so it runs straight from a file:// double-click — no modules, no bundler, no server.
A headless harness (node test/smoke.js) loads the engine and all five modes, running each through setup + 600 physics steps + a render to catch integration bugs.

run.label: Play it instantly
run.code: open index.html   # just double-click — no install, no build

run.label: Or serve the folder
run.code: python3 -m http.server   # then visit the printed URL

repos.name: lattice
repos.tagline: A spreadsheet, rebuilt from first principles in Rust — a fast sparse calc engine with Excel-accurate formulas and dependency-ordered recalculation.
repos.summary: lattice is a spreadsheet system written from scratch: the cell model, A1 addressing, a real formula language (lexer → parser → evaluator), a dependency graph that drives automatic recalculation, around sixty functions, and the file formats that let a workbook survive being closed. The calculation engine is pure Rust and knows nothing about pixels, so it is tested entirely on its own; on top sits a Tauri desktop app with a virtualized canvas grid that feels like the spreadsheets you already know.

screenshots.alt: The lattice spreadsheet grid with a formula bar and selection

howItWorks: sheet-core (pure Rust, no UI): the sparse Cell/Sheet/Workbook model, A1 addressing with $ markers, the value system, and the formula pipeline lexer → parser (precedence climbing) → AST → eval.
Excel-accurate to the quirks: -2^2 is 4, 2^3^2 is 64, IF / IFERROR are lazy, and the full error set (#DIV/0!, #VALUE!, #NAME?, …) propagates exactly as it should.
Dependency-ordered recalculation: extract each formula's precedents, topologically sort with Kahn's algorithm, recompute in order so every cell reads fresh inputs — with circular-reference detection.
sheet-io reads and writes real CSV, .xlsx, OpenDocument .ods and a native JSON format — genuine files other apps open without complaint.
A Tauri desktop shell draws a virtualized canvas grid: only the visible cells render, so scrolling a large sheet stays smooth.

run.label: Run the desktop app
run.code: cargo tauri dev   # needs Rust + Tauri prerequisites

repos.name: ferric
repos.tagline: A word processor in the shape of a limited Microsoft Word — that exports real files you can open anywhere: .docx, .odt, and a paginated PDF.
repos.summary: Headings, fonts and sizes, bold/italic/underline, lists, alignment and live word counts — over a Word-style ribbon. The page you type on is glass; the machinery underneath is iron. ferric-core is pure Rust with no dependency on the UI: it owns the document model and every file-format conversion, and its exports are genuine files the OS recognises as PDF, OpenDocument Text and Microsoft Word 2007+ — not renamed look-alikes.

screenshots.alt: The ferric word processor — a Word-style ribbon with font controls over a paper page

howItWorks: Three clean layers: ferric-core (pure-Rust engine — model + every format), a thin Tauri shell exposing four commands, and a web UI that is a Word-like ribbon over a contenteditable page.
The engine and UI exchange exactly one thing — a Document as JSON: paragraphs of styled runs. The UI never parses a file format; the engine never touches the DOM.
Exports are built in Rust: Office Open XML via docx-rs, OpenDocument XML zipped by hand, and a paginated PDF laid out with printpdf (word-wrapping, per-run fonts, headings, lists, page breaks).
Proof, not promises: the OS 'file' tool recognises each export, and a 12-test suite asserts the headers and round-trips on every build.
Print / Save-as-PDF uses the system print engine via the webview, so the printout is exactly the page you see.

run.label: Run the desktop app
run.code: cargo tauri dev   # needs Rust + Tauri prerequisites

run.label: Write sample files in every format

repos.name: omni-extract
repos.tagline: Feed it any file — a scanned PDF, a phone photo of a receipt, a Word doc, an email, a zip of junk — and get back all the text it can recover. Fully offline.
repos.summary: "Just get the text out of the file" sounds like a one-liner; it is not. A .docx is a zip of XML, a PDF might be selectable text or just a photograph of a page, a .txt won't tell you its encoding, and a screenshot is only pixels until OCR reads it. omni-extract is several tools in a trench coat: a file-type detective that doesn't trust extensions, a router, a dozen extractors, a Tesseract OCR engine for the pixels, recursion for archives, and a normaliser. The core has zero third-party dependencies; nothing ever leaves your machine.
repos.platform: CLI + local browser UI — macOS · Linux · Windows · Docker

screenshots.alt: The omni-extract desktop interface — a drag-and-drop zone over a calm, Apple-style dark layout

howItWorks: The pipeline: file → DETECT → ROUTE → EXTRACT (with a fallback chain) → NORMALIZE → a uniform Document of text + metadata.

Detect by magic-byte signatures, then container sniffing for ambiguous cases (a PK\x03\x04 file could be docx/xlsx/pptx/epub/zip) — only the first 64 KB are read, so detection is O(1) even on a multi-gigabyte file.
The hybrid-PDF trick: read each page's embedded text first and send only the empty (scanned) pages to OCR — a 500-page digital PDF never wakes the OCR engine; a scanned one is fully recovered.
Everything except PDFs and image-OCR runs on the Python standard library alone; OCR is Tesseract's LSTM neural network, PDFs use PyMuPDF.
Built for scale: parallel batch across files, lazy imports, a content-hash cache, per-file timeouts and archive-bomb guards. Every result records which backend ran, so nothing is ever silently faked.

run.label: Fully self-contained via Docker
run.code: docker build -t omni-extract . && docker run --rm -v "$PWD:/data" omni-extract scan.pdf

repos.name: hello-world-showcase
repos.tagline: A full-screen wall of 41 programming languages all saying the same thing — running for real on your own machine. One button in the centre ends it all.
repos.summary: A small, honest demonstration. The controller runs each language's Hello World program once, captures the output it actually produced, and shows every result together on a single full-screen page. Languages whose runtime isn't installed are shown too — clearly marked 'runtime not installed' rather than faked. Pure Python 3, no third-party packages, on macOS, Linux and Windows. Click END PROJECT and the whole thing shuts down: server gone, browser window gone, temp files gone.

screenshots.alt: A wall of programming languages each printing Hello World, with an END PROJECT button glowing in the centre

howItWorks: controller/languages.py is the registry — one Lang(...) entry per language with its source, build/run commands, required executables, timeout and a historical credit. Adding a language is a single entry.
controller/runner.py runs each language once in its own private temp dir, isolated as a process group, with a wall-clock timeout; on timeout the entire process tree is killed.
Results are collected in memory and rendered by controller/page.py into one self-contained HTML page — no external CSS, JS or network.
controller/server.py is a tiny localhost-only server with two routes: / returns the page, /exit triggers a clean shutdown that kills the browser, deletes the temp dir and stops the server.
Every tile is honest: 🟢 LIVE ran for real, 🟡 FAIL shows the error, ⚪ N/A means the runtime isn't installed — never faked.

run.label: Run it (pure standard library)
run.code: git clone https://github.com/danieldevelopes-collab/hello-world-showcase.git && cd hello-world-showcase && python3 run.py

run.label: See which languages will light up
